personal-cookbook.title: Build a Personal Cookbook
personal-cookbook.tagline: Keep the recipes you actually cook
personal-cookbook.description: Turn your WordPress into a recipe box for weeknight staples, family favorites, and dishes you want to repeat. Cookbook stores ingredients, steps, prep and cook times, and can pull a recipe in from a URL so you can keep the good version on your own site.

steps.title: Install Cookbook
steps.description: Cookbook adds a dedicated recipe app to your WordPress with fields for ingredients, instructions, and timing.

steps.title: Add one trusted recipe
steps.description: Start with something you already cook from memory. Writing down the ingredients, steps, prep time, and cook time gives the rest of your cookbook a useful template.

steps.title: Import the recipes you keep searching for
steps.description: Paste a recipe URL into Cookbook to bring it into your WordPress. Once it's saved, you can adjust the wording, timing, and ingredients to match how you actually make it.

steps.title: Write kitchen notes after you cook
steps.description: After making a recipe, update it with the substitutions, serving notes, or timing changes that worked. The value grows when the recipe reflects your kitchen, not the original page.

customize-with-ai.title: Build Your Dream App
customize-with-ai.tagline: Describe the app you wish existed
customize-with-ai.description: Use the AI Assistant to turn a specific workflow into a small WordPress app: a tracker, directory, checklist, calculator, archive, dashboard, or something entirely custom. It can start from scratch or, when useful, connect to recipes, notes, contacts, saved articles, posts, or other data already on your site.

steps.title: Install the AI Assistant
steps.description: Adds a chat panel where you can ask the model to create custom WordPress features and apps.

steps.title: Connect a model
steps.description: Add an API key for Claude, OpenAI, or Gemini, or point it at LM Studio / Ollama running locally. Building custom features needs a model connected before the assistant can make changes.

steps.title: Pick one useful workflow
steps.description: Choose a small, concrete app you would actually use: a packing list, gift tracker, habit log, garden planner, invoice helper, research board, or dashboard. It does not need existing site data to be worthwhile.

steps.title: Describe what it should do
steps.description: Tell the assistant the screens, fields, and actions you want. If the app should use data already in WordPress, say which data; otherwise ask it to create the structure it needs.

steps.title: Test and refine it
steps.description: Use the first version with real entries, then ask the assistant for the missing pieces: a better list view, filters, custom fields, import support, or a dashboard widget. The app gets useful when it fits the way you actually work.

steps.title: Share the app
steps.description: When the app feels ready, download it as a ZIP file so someone else can install it on their own WordPress. Keep refining your copy and share a fresh ZIP whenever you make a better version.

research-tool.title: Research Tool
research-tool.tagline: Look up sources, keep the useful parts
research-tool.description: Use your WordPress as a focused research workspace. Search Wikipedia from your own site, save the articles and snippets you want to return to, and clip web sources into your archive so project notes and references stay together.

steps.title: Install Wordopedia
steps.description: Wordopedia lets you search, browse, and save Wikipedia articles and snippets without leaving your WordPress.

steps.title: Save a starting set of articles
steps.description: Search for the people, places, concepts, or events around your research topic and save the articles or snippets you expect to revisit.

steps.title: Collect supporting sources
steps.description: Post Collection saves articles from the wider web into your WordPress, so sources outside Wikipedia can sit beside your saved reference material.

steps.title: Write notes around the sources
steps.description: Create a post for the research question or project and link to the saved articles and collected sources as you work. The value comes from keeping the source list close to your own notes.

family-blog.title: Build a Family Blog
family-blog.tagline: Memories worth keeping, kept where they belong
family-blog.description: A private blog for your family — stories, milestones, and photos pulled in from group chats. Anyone in the family can post from their phone, and the whole archive is preserved by the same backups that keep your WordPress alive.

steps.title: Write the first post
steps.description: Don't overthink the setup — write something. Even just "hello, this is where we'll keep things" sets the tone. Once one post exists, everything else makes more sense.

steps.title: Make the site private
steps.description: Network Privacy adds a "visible only to subscribers" option in Settings → Reading. Invite family members as subscribers and the rest of the internet can't see in.

steps.title: Let everyone post from their phone
steps.description: Enable Mastodon Apps turns your WordPress into a Mastodon-compatible server. Family members can use any Mastodon app to post photos and short notes — no learning WordPress required.

steps.title: Save photos before chats forget them
steps.description: Chat to Blog imports photos from your group chats directly into posts — no forwarding, no downloading. The memories outlive the messaging service.

steps.title: Make it last with a static archive
steps.description: Static Archive writes an HTML copy of every post into your uploads folder, right next to the photos, and regenerates automatically on every change. On my.wordpress.net the platform's automatic site backups already include the uploads folder, so the static archive rides along — even a future restore without a running WordPress is a browsable archive.

family-history.title: Document Family History
family-history.tagline: A private Wikipedia for your family
family-history.description: Build your own Wikipedia about the people in your family — who they were, where they lived, how they connect. The Family Wiki plugin ships with a pre-defined set of Person fields (dates, relations, places), so once you install it the Wikipedia-style structure is already there. You just write.

steps.title: Turn your site into a wiki
steps.description: Family Wiki adds nested pages, cross-linking, and shared editing — the structure you need to write about people who connect to each other.

steps.title: Add the Person fields engine
steps.description: Family Wiki bundles a ready-made set of Person fields (alive, born, died, parents, children, places). Secure Custom Fields is the engine that loads them — once it's active, every wiki page gets the fields automatically. ACF works the same way if you already use it.

steps.title: Set a static homepage as your family tree
steps.description: Settings → Reading → "A static page" → create a new page that lists the closest relatives you want to write about first. Don't aim for completeness — start with the handful you have stories for, link each name to their wiki entry, and grow it from there. This page becomes the index everyone navigates from.

steps.title: Write the first profile
steps.description: Pick one relative — probably the one with the most stories — and write their page. Fill in the structured fields, link to anyone they connect to. Drop the [name_with_bio] shortcode at the top of the page to auto-generate a one-liner from the fields. The next profiles get easier once the pattern is set.

steps.title: Make it private (recommended)
steps.description: Family Wiki adds its own option in Settings → Reading: "I would like my site to be private, visible only to myself and users I choose." Turn it on and family history stays in the family — only logged-in users you've invited can read or edit.

steps.title: Add a family calendar
steps.description: Family Wiki includes a Family Calendar block (and a Birthday Calendar block for living relatives) — drop one onto a page and it surfaces every date stored in the wiki. A nice way to remember the next birthday or anniversary.

take-notes.title: Take Notes in WordPress
take-notes.tagline: Capture notes in the shape that fits
take-notes.description: There are many ways to take notes in WordPress: a plain post, a page, or a dedicated note app. On my.wordpress.net, your WordPress is private, so even a regular post can work as a personal note; add Memex when you want linked notes, backlinks, daily notes, tags, or reminders.

steps.title: Start with a regular post
steps.description: Write a post for today's notes, a meeting, a link dump, or a running project log. On my.wordpress.net your WordPress is private, so a normal post can work as a personal note without extra setup.

steps.title: Use Memex for linked notes
steps.description: Memex is optional. Install it if you want a dedicated note-taking app with daily notes, tags, reminders, bi-directional links, and automatic backlinks.

steps.title: Link related ideas as you write
steps.description: In Memex, use [[wiki-links]] for people, projects, topics, and recurring questions. Memex creates backlinks automatically, so each note becomes an entry point into the notes around it.

steps.title: Add reminders where follow-up matters
steps.description: Turn notes into future prompts when a thought needs action later. Reminders keep follow-up close to the context that created it.

steps.title: Import notes from another app
steps.description: Bring in existing notes from Obsidian, Notion, Evernote, or Roam Research when you're ready. Start with a small import first so you can review how the notes land before moving everything.

steps.title: Preserve notes as a static archive
steps.description: Static Archive writes HTML copies of your posts into uploads, so your notes stay browsable from a backup even if WordPress is not running.

reading-hub.title: Personal Reading Hub
reading-hub.tagline: Follow friends, save articles, read on your terms
reading-hub.description: Subscribe to RSS, Atom, and ActivityPub feeds in one place. Clip articles from around the web to read later, and send the long ones to your e-reader.

steps.title: Install the reader
steps.description: The Friends plugin is your private feed reader. It handles RSS, Atom, and ActivityPub — including following Mastodon accounts.

steps.title: Subscribe to your first feeds
steps.description: Paste a few feed URLs, or follow a Mastodon handle like @alex@kirk.at. Your timeline lives on your site, not a third-party app.

steps.title: Save articles to read later
steps.description: Post Collection clips any web article into your WordPress so you keep a copy, even if the original goes away.

steps.title: Send long reads to your e-reader
steps.description: Push posts to your Kindle or Kobo as ePub or by email. Read in peace, away from the browser.

bring-your-data.title: Bring Your Data In
bring-your-data.tagline: Move existing data onto your WordPress, in the right shape for each app
bring-your-data.description: Do you already have content elsewhere and managed to get them exported?. WordPress has built-in importers for the standard formats; the AI Assistant handles everything messier by reading what you paste in and writing it as the right kind of record for the right app.

steps.title: Try the built-in importers first
steps.description: Tools → Import ships with plug-and-play importers for WordPress XML exports, RSS feeds, Blogger, Tumblr, LiveJournal, and a few more. If your old platform exports any of these formats, this is the cleanest path — structure, dates, and authors come across intact.

steps.title: Install the AI Assistant for messier sources
steps.description: The AI Assistant reads the data shape of every app you've installed and writes records in their native format — CRM contacts, wiki Person pages, feed subscriptions, post clippings — not generic blog posts.

steps.title: Connect a model
steps.description: Add an API key for Claude, OpenAI, or Gemini, or point the AI Assistant at LM Studio / Ollama running locally. The catch for now: the AI Assistant doesn't ship with a built-in model, so you bring your own. A local LLM is free but slower; cloud APIs cost a few cents per import session.

steps.title: Paste in your stuff and tell it where to land
steps.description: Paste a chunk of text, drop a file, or describe what you have — then tell the assistant which app it belongs in. "Add these to my CRM." "Make wiki pages for these relatives." "Subscribe my reader to these feeds." It shows you what it's about to write before saving anything, so you end up with reviewed drafts in the right app rather than a generic pile of blog posts.

stay-in-touch.title: Stay in Touch
stay-in-touch.tagline: A private contact directory with reminders
stay-in-touch.description: Track the people who matter to you and get nudges to reach out before too much time passes. Your relationships, your data — never sold, never mined.

steps.title: Install Personal CRM
steps.description: Adds a contacts directory inside your WordPress. Name, notes, last contact date, custom fields — all yours.

steps.title: Add the people who matter
steps.description: Start with the ten or twenty you actually want to keep up with. The point isn't a complete address book — it's the ones you'd hate to drift away from.

steps.title: Set a check-in cadence
steps.description: Pick a reminder interval per contact (a month, a quarter, a year). When the date comes around, you'll see them on the dashboard with a nudge to reach out.
